coredata.dc:title: Green emitting carbon dots-immunosensor on magnetic nanoparticles for detection of Nanog antigen as a cancer stem cell biomarker
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Mehrabi, Fatemeh
Hosseini, Morteza
Sadeghi, Niloufar
Mohammadi, Javad
Ganjali, Mohammad reza
Ranjbar, Bijan
coredata.dc:description:
Background Embryonic Nanog is recognized as a crucial controller of pluripotency. In the progression of cancer and the formation of metastasis, cancer cells with stem cell-like characteristics frequently express Nanog. Precisely identifying the Nanog antigen poses a significant challenge due to its low abundance in biofluids. The precise detection of the Nanog antigen originating from cancer cells has attracted growing interest for its potential uses in diagnostics and prognostics.
Results In this study, a novel fluorescence strategy utilizing green carbon dots was developed for the highly sensitive and specific detection of Nanog. This approach involved the use of fluoro-immunosensors based on magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) and antibodies targeting the cancer Stem Cells (CSCs) biomarker, Nanog. In this study, the targeted Nanog was magnetically separated following its reaction with green carbon dots and magnetic nanoparticles, both conjugated with anti-nanog antibodies. The findings show a clear increase in fluorescence with the rising concentration of Nanog antigen in the sample. The linear range for Nanog, measured under optimal experimental conditions, was found to be 5.0 × 10 −11 g/L to 1.0 × 10 −9 g/L. The detection limit (LOD) was calculated to be 1.0 × 10 −11 g/L.
Significance This study introduces a fluoro-immunosensor employing magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) and high quantum efficiency green-emitting carbon dots. This represents the first use of these carbon dots in this type of sensor. The biosensor has demonstrated effective detection of Nanog in biological samples. This developed biosensor, which is both convenient and highly sensitive, presents a significant opportunity for quantifying Nanog in biological research and clinical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Immunoassay
Green-emitting carbon dots
Magnetic nanoparticles
Nanog

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of highly fluorescent green carbon dots and their conjugation to antibodies
Fabrication of magnetic nanoparticles and antibody immobilization
Characterization of green carbon dots and CDs-conjugated anti nanog antibodies
Optimization of CD concentration and incubation time
Analysis of nanog in clinical urine samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The absorbance spectrum of CDs (2 g/L, PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.4)), (Abs, green dash) CDs-Ab (Abs, orange solid), and Ab (Abs, black dots) in UV–Vis. Insets: The pictures were taken with UV light at 365 nm and centered around CDs, CDs-Ab, and Ab aqueous solution. (B) The fluorescence emission spectrum of green CDs (with excitation wavelengths increasing from 300 nm to 420 nm). (C) emission spectrum of CDs and CDs-Ab (λex = 400 nm). (D) (a) The TEM image and (b) size distribution of the green-emitting CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
FT-IR spectrum of green-emitting CDs and CDs-Ab. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

In Vitro Detection of Nanog Using a Fluorescent Immunosensor. (A) Fluorescent immunosensor response to various concentrations of Nanog. (B) Linear relationship between fluorescence signal intensity and Nanog concentration over a range of 5.0 × 10−11 to 1.0 × 10−9 g/L.
Fluorescence immunoassay selectivity was assessed using an antigen concentration of 10 × 10 −11 g/L, CD concentration of 2 g/L, and MNP concentration of 0.1 g/L.

Fluorescence spectra of the urine of a CSC patient (positive), the PBS as a normal control (blank), and the urine of healthy person (negative), respectively, inset shows the image of visual detecting samples under 365 nm UV light.
Schematic Representation of a Sandwich Fluoro-Immunosensor Using MNPs for Nanog Antigen Assay.

head.ce:title: Green emitting carbon dots-immunosensor on magnetic nanoparticles for detection of Nanog antigen as a cancer stem cell biomarker

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Nanobiotechnology, Faculty of Biological Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Embryonic Nanog is recognized as a crucial controller of pluripotency. In the progression of cancer and the formation of metastasis, cancer cells with stem cell-like characteristics frequently express Nanog. Precisely identifying the Nanog antigen poses a significant challenge due to its low abundance in biofluids. The precise detection of the Nanog antigen originating from cancer cells has attracted growing interest for its potential uses in diagnostics and prognostics.

In this study, a novel fluorescence strategy utilizing green carbon dots was developed for the highly sensitive and specific detection of Nanog. This approach involved the use of fluoro-immunosensors based on magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) and antibodies targeting the cancer Stem Cells (CSCs) biomarker, Nanog. In this study, the targeted Nanog was magnetically separated following its reaction with green carbon dots and magnetic nanoparticles, both conjugated with anti-nanog antibodies. The findings show a clear increase in fluorescence with the rising concentration of Nanog antigen in the sample. The linear range for Nanog, measured under optimal experimental conditions, was found to be 5.0 × 10 −11 g/L to 1.0 × 10 −9 g/L. The detection limit (LOD) was calculated to be 1.0 × 10 −11 g/L.

This study introduces a fluoro-immunosensor employing magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) and high quantum efficiency green-emitting carbon dots. This represents the first use of these carbon dots in this type of sensor. The biosensor has demonstrated effective detection of Nanog in biological samples. This developed biosensor, which is both convenient and highly sensitive, presents a significant opportunity for quantifying Nanog in biological research and clinical applications.

ce:simple-para.ce:bold: Green emitting carbon dots-immunosensor on magnetic beads for detection of nanog antigen as a cancer stem cell biomarker.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanog antigen is a key homeobox transcription factor that supports the pluripotency and self-renewal of embryonic stem cells [1]. This essential transcription factor interacts with DNA and plays a critical role in regulating human development by controlling various processes [2]. After birth, Nanog expression is typically suppressed or absent in the majority of tissues [3]. Overexpression of Nanog enhances clonogenic potential and promotes tumor formation [4]. Transcription factors, such as Nanog, are expressed in cancer stem cells (CSCs) or tumor-initiating cells, with expression levels varying across different tumor types. Across various tumor types, cancer stem cells (CSCs) consistently display the upregulation of these stemness genes. [5,6]. Accurate and precise methods for detecting Nanog are essential given its crucial role in regulating cell pluripotency and driving germ cell cancer development. The analysis of Nanog transcription at the RNA level can be achieved through quantitative PCR techniques. To correlate Nanog transcript expression with protein detection, researchers commonly use techniques like Western blotting [7], immunocytochemistry (ICC) [8], immunofluorescence (IF) [9], and immunohistochemistry [10]. While existing techniques lack the capacity for absolute Nanog quantification, relative measurement can be achieved through a TaqMan protein assay that employs PCR amplification of an oligonucleotide label [11]. Nanog quantification can also be done by Human Nanog ELISA Kit for the quantitative measurement of Human Nanog in Cell culture extract samples [12].

Fluorescence immunoassays (FIAs) have gained increasing importance in recent years [13,14]. These assays rely on the specific binding of antigens and antibodies, with fluorescent labels used for detection. In traditional immunoassays, antibodies are often linked to fluorophores or enzymes to translate target binding into detectable signals, using fluorescence or colorimetry [15,16]. One prominent FIA technique is the sandwich fluoro-immunoassay, widely employed in biomedical and research settings for detecting various compounds or analytes within samples [17]. In typical fluorescent immunoassays, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) are often used as probes. However, making QDs usually requires strict conditions and involves heavy metals like cadmium and lead. It also typically needs several steps to exchange hydrophilic ligands [18,19].

Carbon dots (CDs) are nanoparticles that have adjustable physical and optical properties [20]. Their impressive features, such as intense brightness, minimal toxicity, adaptability in altering surface properties, exceptional durability against fading, excellent compatibility with biological systems, and remarkable ability to dissolve in water, continue to captivate humanity [21–24]. Furthermore, their exceptional properties have contributed to significant advancements in both the synthesis and applications of CDs [25]. CDs are increasingly recognized for their applications in fluorescence immunoassays across various biomedical fields, including biosensing, cancer cell imaging, drug delivery, and monitoring therapeutic responses [26–29].

magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs), also known as superparamagnetic particles, are used in biosensing to isolate biomolecules, offering a simple and efficient method for doing so. MNPs, modified with diverse recognition elements, enable selective capture of various molecules based on their bio-affinity [30]. Antibodies can be attached to magnetic nanoparticles either through a direct covalent bond or through specific antibody-binding proteins, and the captured biomolecules on the MNP surface can then act as a biorecognition layer for interactions with other molecules [31,32].

Given the significant role of Nanog and FIA in cancer biology, we undertook an analysis of the Nanog antigen in clinical urine samples of cancer patients through fabrication of a fluorescence immunosensor. This investigation aims to explore the potential of Nanog as a diagnostic marker for cancer, offering a promising avenue for early detection and monitoring of the disease.

In this study, we present a fluorescent immunoassay sensing platform for the detection of Nanog by utilizing magnetic nanoparticles. This performed using two antibodies, Ab1 and Ab2. The detector antibody (anti-Nanog) (Ab2) was labeled using green carbon dots thorough EDC/NHS functionalization. These green carbon dots were synthesized using a straightforward solid-state reaction method and exhibited excellent fluorescence properties, colloidal stability, and dispersibility. Notably, they demonstrated a remarkable quantum efficiency of up to 45.5 % when excited at 400 nm. On the other hand, APTES-functionalized magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) were prepared and subsequently modified with an anti-Nanog antibody (Ab1). These modified MNPs served as capture probes in a sandwich immunoassay, effectively reducing the time required for magnetic separation. Utilizing functionalized carbon dots (CDs) and magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs), a novel fluorescent immunoassay has been developed, offering a powerful tool for detecting the Nanog tumor marker. This assay demonstrates exceptional sensitivity, achieving a limit of detection (LOD) of 1.0 × 10 −11 g/L. The method offers an easy and direct signal generation mechanism, enabling rapid and sensitive monitoring of Nanog residues in the urine of patients with cancer stem cells (CSCs). Furthermore, the study expands the application of green light-emitting carbon dots (CDs) as probes in immunoassays.

N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4), APTES (3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane), 99 % purity, Iron (II) chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2·4H2O), ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH), urea, tri-sodium citrate dihydrate.

Monoclonal anti-Nanog antibody (mouse origin), 1.3 g/L concentration (Ab1 and Ab2), which identify the Nanog antigen, were bought from Padzacompany (Iran). These biomolecules were sourced from Padzacompany in Iran: Receptor tyrosine kinase-like orphan receptor 1 (ROR1), Human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2), Prostate-specific antigen (PSA). Antigen and antibody were prepared and diluted in PBS 0.1 M buffer, pH 7.4. Ethical approval (A.1402.11.29.EC.NIGEB.IR) was obtained from the National Institute of Genetic Engineering and Biotechnology for the use of human urine samples in this research.

To measure the photoluminescence spectrum, the PerkinElmer LS-55 luminescence spectrometer from the USA was employed; A PerkinElmer Lambda 25 spectrometer was used to acquire absorption spectra. A PerkinElmer Lambda 55 spectrophotometer was employed to measure the fluorescence spectra. A CM120 TEM was used to perform transmission electron microscopy (TEM) with an accelerating voltage of 100 kV. For TEM analysis, a drop of colloidal solution was placed on a carbon-coated copper grid and left to dry at room temperature. The morphology of the magnetic nanoparticles was observed using a TESCAN MIRA3 field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) from the Czech Republic.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of highly fluorescent green carbon dots and their conjugation to antibodies
ce:section.ce:para: In the course of conducting chemical synthesis, tri-sodium citrate (0.2 g) and urea (0.2 g) were ground evenly using an agate mortar. For 2.5 h in an oven with ambient conditions, the resulting mixture was heated at 180 °C. After the reaction was finished, the resulting substance was dissolved in water. To remove any large particles and purify the solution, it was subjected to high-speed centrifugation (12000 rpm for 10 min). Afterward, Pure CDs were obtained by dialysis with a 500–1000 Da cellulose ester for 12 h after the solution was further filtered through a 0.22 m filter membrane. To characterize the solution of CDs, it was dispersed in deionized water [33,34]. To fully harness the potential of CDs as biocompatible fluorescent labels, they are frequently connected to biomolecules using bifunctional linkers like EDC and NHS [35]. To attach antibodies to the functional groups of CDs, EDC and NHS were used to induce a bioconjugation reaction. Antibody activation was achieved by incubating 80 μL of a 1 g/L antibody solution with 8 mg each of NHS and EDC dissolved in 1 mL of 0.1 M PBS buffer at pH 7.4 for 30 min. To create the CD-antibody complexes, the activated antibodies underwent an incubation process with 0.4 mL of CDs (3 g/L) at 37 °C for 2 h. [36]. In this procedure, CD nanoparticles and Nanog antibodies were linked together through strong amide bonds created between the amine groups of CDs and the carboxylic acid groups of antibodies. Before use, the dialysis-treated solution was stored at −20 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of magnetic nanoparticles and antibody immobilization
ce:section.ce:para: To perform this method, 0.3125 g of FeCl2.4H2O was dissolved in 10 mL of distilled water, and the mixture was stirred until a uniform solution was achieved. To the continuously stirring solution, 1.6 mL of 25 % ammonia solution was added, Permitting the conversion of Fe (II) to a higher oxidation state. The solution was then stirred for 10 min before adding 0.625 mL of APTES, followed by another 10 min of stirring. The reaction mixture was autoclaved in a sealed pressure vessel with a volume of 50 mL at a temperature of 134 °C for 3 h. After the completion of the reaction, the mixture was allowed to cool down to room temperature. A black precipitate was formed, which was subsequently washed multiple times with water and ethanol to eliminate any excess reactants. The resulting compound was then separated using a magnetic field and dried in a hot air oven at 80 °C for 7–8 h [37]. To covalently bind the carboxyl groups (-COOH) of the antibodies (Ab) to the surface of the magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs), a surface functionalization process was carried out. This involved introducing amine groups (-NH2) onto the surface of the MNPs. For this purpose, APTES-Fe3O4 was utilized. A customized EDC/NHS coupling method has been used to modify the antibody to adhere to a surface that has been treated with amino groups [38]. To activate the antibody, we first dissolved 9 mg of NHS and 5 mg of EDC in 1 mL of PBS buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.4). This solution was then added to 1 mL of MNPs (0.1 g/L) suspended in PBS buffer. Subsequently, 80 μL of antibodies (1 g/L) were introduced and the mixture was gently stirred for 8 h in an ice bath. This method effectively bonded the antibodies to the APTES-Fe3O4 nanoparticles' surface [32,39].

To determine the quantum yield (Φ) of the CDs, the integrated photoluminescence intensities and absorbance values were compared to those of rhodamine 6G. Rhodamine 6G, known to have a relative quantum yield of 95 % in ethanol, was used as a standard fluorescent reference [34].
Q
Y T
= Q
Y R
A R
A T
I T
I R
(
η T
η R
)
2

The quantum yield (Φ) of carbon dots (CDs) was determined by comparing their integrated photoluminescence intensities (I) and absorbance values (A) to those of a reference fluorophore (denoted as subscript R) with a known quantum yield. The refractive index (η) was also taken into account in the calculations.
Adult volunteers who had cancer stem cells provided human urine samples. Before analysis, these samples were placed in a freezer at −20 °C and diluted 2.5 × 104 fold by PBS (pH 7.4).

Sandwich immunoassays are commonly employed to detect various disease biomarkers and small molecules [40] A novel fluorescence immunoassay (FIA) platform utilizing magnetic nanoparticles has been developed for the detection of Nanog antigen in CSCs. The mixture was prepared by combining 200 μL of APTES-Fe3O4-Ab1 and 50 μL of 1.0 × 10 −9 g/L Nanog antigen solution, followed by incubation in an ice bath for 15 min. For the formation of CD-Ab2-Nanog Ag immune complexes, a 200 μL volume of CD-Ab2 (anti-Nanog) suspension was mixed with the above suspension in an ice bath for 15 min. After 30 min, the separation process was conducted with the assistance of a magnetic field. The complex was successfully separated, and the supernatant was subsequently discarded. Using an LS-55 luminescence spectrometer, the PL spectrum was immediately recorded after resuspending the suspension in 400 μL of PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.4) and transferring it to a 1 cm path length quartz cell.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of green carbon dots and CDs-conjugated anti nanog antibodies

ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S1A depicts the UV–Vis spectral data for a 1.0 g/L solution of APTES-Fe3O4 nanoparticles. The peak located at 406 ± 0.88 nm corresponds to the presence of APTES-Fe3O4 NPs. These nanoparticles possess magnetic properties, as shown in Fig. S1B. In this case, the use of a non-magnetic coating, specifically APTES, facilitates the binding of antibodies to it. To gain insights into the structure of APTES-Fe3O4 NPs, the FTIR spectrum was recorded and is presented in Fig. S2. These results proved that the antibodies were successfully conjugated to the MNPs. The Fe–O bonds showed up at 524 cm−1, and that's due to stretching vibrations. Additionally, vibrations of Fe–O–H and O–H on the surface of Fe3O4 were observed at 859 cm−1. The presence of Si–O groups was indicated by asymmetric stretching vibrations at 1077 and 1160 cm−1 [46]. Furthermore, a distinctive broad peak ranging from 3200 to 3600 cm−1 was observed, possibly indicating the presence of hydroxyl and amine groups. The presence of a peak at 1650 cm−1, attributed to the CO bond in the antibody amide I, provided crucial evidence for the successful conjugation of the antibody to the nanoparticles. Additionally, A peak observed at 1403 cm−1 was attributed to the N–H stretching vibration of the antibody amide II band [47]. Characteristic CH2 stretching vibrations were observed at 2922 and 2850 cm−1 indicating the binding of APTES molecules to the surface of magnetite, forming APTES-MNP. This observation confirms the successful attachment of APTES to the magnetite surface [48]. These findings confirmed the interaction between silane molecules on the surface of Fe3O4 nanoparticles and the covalent binding of antibodies to the MNP surface. The surface morphology of APTES-Fe3O4 NPs was examined through FE-SEM images, revealing particles that displayed a consistent spherical shape and size. Fig. S3 showcases an image of the APTES-Fe3O4 NPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of CD concentration and incubation time
ce:section.ce:para: To optimize the detection performance for Nanog, various experimental parameters in the detection protocol were fine-tuned. These parameters included the concentration of carbon dots (CDs), the temperature and reaction time for the binding between Nanog and complexes, the concentration of magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs), and the pH level. Since the fluorescence tag plays a crucial role in this assay, the optimal concentration of 2 g/L was obtained for it (Fig. S4a). Afterward, the incubation time and reaction temperature between Nanog and the complex were examined. As the incubation time increased, the fluorescence signal gradually increased and reached its maximum when the reaction temperature was set to 10 °C with an incubation time of 30 min (Fig. S4b). Subsequently, the concentration of MNPs and the pH level were also optimized. The optimal signal ratio was achieved with a volume of 200 μL, a concentration of 0.1 g/L for MNPs, and a pH level of 7.4, as illustrated in Fig. S4c and Fig. S4d, respectively.

under the optimal conditions, the suggested method was utilized to detect Nanog, as depicted in Scheme 1 . As depicted in Fig. 3 A, the fluorescence intensity rises as the Nanog concentrations increase from 5.0 × 10 −11 g/L to 1.0 × 10 −9 g/L. In Fig. 3A, we observe the correlation between the intensity of CD emission and the concentration of Nanog. Fig. 3B demonstrates that the fluorescence intensity exhibits a linear increase within the Nanog concentration range of 5.0 × 10 −11 g/L – 1.0 × 10 −9 g/L. The correlation equation, Intensity = 379.06x + 599 (R2 = 0.9848), has been established. Using the 3σ/slope formula, the estimated limit of detection (LOD) for Nanog is 1.0 × 10 −11 g/L. The slope of the regression line (m) is the sensitivity of the biosensor. The slope (m) of the line in this assay (Intensity = 379.06x + 599) is = 379.06)Intensity/gL−1).

Additionally, the linear range and LOD of this method were compared to those of other established methods. As shown in Table S1, the LOD of the developed assay aligns closely with the majority of other methods listed in the table [49–52].

Under consistent experimental conditions, with six identical volumes and concentrations of APTES-Fe3O4-Ab1 and CD-Ab2, the method successfully detected three different concentrations of Nanog antigen: (10 × 10−11 g/L, 50 × 10−11 g/L, and 90 × 10−11 g/L). The results showed RSDs of 3.12 %, 2.34 % and 0.87 % (Fig. S5), results indicate excellent reproducibility of the method.

The sandwich immunoassay exhibited stable performance, maintaining sensitivity for six weeks (monitored weekly with 50 × 10−11 g/L target molecule additions), indicating probe stability; however, signal intensity decreased by 10–15 % thereafter.

To assess the effectiveness of a sensing system, it is crucial to evaluate its ability to selectively respond to the target in the presence of potential interferences. Therefore, the probe's ability to distinguish Nanog from various antibodies was assessed. The diagnostic platform with Nanog antibody was used to assess the reactivity of other antigens like PSA, ROR1, and HER2. Each antigen was tested at a concentration of 10 × 10 −11 g/L. In contrast to the Nanog antigen, these solutions notably decreased the fluorescence intensity This finding indicates the method's specificity for the Nanog antigen (Fig. 4 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of nanog in clinical urine samples
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescent probe's ability to analyze real samples was evaluated using urine samples collected from a local hospital in Tehran, Iran. Three sets of positive, negative, and blank samples were assessed. 2 mL of urine samples were collected in sterile plastic containers, stored at 10 °C, and analyzed within 40 min 100 μL of the positive, negative, and blank urine samples were separately combined with 200 μL of the MNPs-Ab1. After a 15-min incubation, 200 μL of CDs-Ab2 were added and incubated for another 15 min. Next, magnetic separation was conducted, followed by three washes with PBS buffer. 400 μL of the sample were moved to the quartz fluorescence cuvette for analysis (Fig. 5
). There were no issues with the photodecomposition of the samples during handling, as the fluorescence of urine analyzed after 2 h and after 40 min yielded nearly identical results.

The fluorescence intensities of the sandwich assay system were recorded at λex = 400 nm in urine samples. As shown in Fig. 5, there was a weak fluorescence response for both blank and negative samples compared to the positive samples. Notably, in the presence of the target in the positive urine sample, the fluorescence intensity and brightness of the assay system were significantly enhanced (Fig. 5, inset). This demonstrates that the fabricated QDs-based fluorescence immunosensor provides a reasonable, feasible, and effective platform for rapid Nanog detection.

To conclude, CDs were synthesized with a quantum efficiency exceeding 45.5 %, incorporating a carboxylic group and a surface-inactive amine group. Next, EDC/NHS-carboxylated CDs were used to form complexes with anti-Nanog, leading to the development of CD-labeled 0antibody conjugates. By incorporating MNPs-Ab1-Nanog into CDs-Ab2, a fluorescence sandwich immunoassay method was established for the quantitative evaluation of human CSC Nanog antigen. The results highlight the effectiveness of the proposed method in Nanog antigen detection, demonstrating a wide range of quantifiable concentrations and a low limit of detection, indicating its high sensitivity. Moreover, Nanog levels in the urine of CSC patients can be evaluated with satisfactory outcomes. This simple and efficient method is anticipated to promote the use of CDs obtained from eco-friendly precursors in fluoro-immunosensors, leading to innovative advancements in immunofluorescence techniques.

Fatemeh Mehrabi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Morteza Hosseini: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Conceptualization. Niloufar Sadeghi: Validation, Resources, Methodology. Javad Mohammadi: Resources, Methodology, Investigation. Mohammad reza Ganjali: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Resources. Bijan Ranjbar: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the Research Council of Tarbiat Modares University, University of Tehran and the Iran National Science Foundation (INSF: (4025683), for the financial support of this work.

Cell Res.

PLoS One

Stem Cell.
